skill.name: azure-resource-lookup
skill.description: List, find, and inspect Azure resources across subscriptions and resource groups using Azure Resource Graph and Azure MCP tools. The skill helps answer inventory, tag, orphaned resource, and resource discovery questions without creating or changing Azure resources.
skill.summary: Discover Azure resources across subscriptions with read-only Resource Graph queries.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command and crypto-like patterns, but review shows the hits are Markdown examples, KQL snippets, and text matches such as description or desc. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, mutation workflow, or malicious intent was found. The remaining risk is operational: the skill can guide execution of Azure CLI read-only inventory queries that may expose tenant metadata to the active AI session.

medium_findings.title: Read-Only Azure Inventory Commands Can Expose Metadata
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally guides users to run Azure Resource Graph queries through Azure CLI or MCP tooling. This is legitimate read-only Azure inventory behavior, but results can include resource names, groups, locations, tags, public network settings, and subscription-level metadata that should be handled as sensitive operational data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external command behavior is clearly documented and semantically tied to the skill purpose. The concern is metadata exposure from legitimate read-only queries, not command injection or malicious execution.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Hits Are Azure CLI Documentation
low_findings.description: The numerous external command findings point to Markdown examples for az graph query and az extension add. They do not show hidden shell execution, dynamic command construction in code, or an attempt to run commands outside the expected Azure lookup workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is inside Markdown tables or fenced examples and matches the stated Azure Resource Graph purpose. No code file or shell wrapper accepts untrusted input for execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The high weak-crypto findings do not correspond to DES, MD5, SHA1, or any cryptographic API. The evidence lines contain frontmatter description text, KQL sort direction text such as desc, and a field name containing shortDescription.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows ordinary documentation and KQL field text, not cryptographic primitives. The static labels are caused by substring matching rather than security-relevant code.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Hit Is Error-Handling Text
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance finding points to documentation for a BadRequest query error and advises checking KQL syntax. It does not instruct host enumeration, operating system probing, network scanning, or privilege discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is an Azure query troubleshooting row, not a reconnaissance command or tactic. Nearby context is limited to Resource Graph error handling.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure CLI Command Generation For Resource Queries
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents generating and running az graph query commands from user intent. This is expected for the skill, but users should scope subscriptions and review generated commands before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible and intentional, and it relies on Azure CLI authentication and permissions. It is not malicious, but it deserves user awareness because it executes commands against cloud resources.

content.value_statement: Azure teams need fast answers about what resources exist and where they are running. This skill turns inventory, tag, health, and orphaned-resource questions into read-only Azure Resource Graph workflows.
actual_capabilities: Routes Azure resource lookup questions to dedicated MCP tools when available.
Generates Azure Resource Graph query workflows for resource inventory questions.
Supports cross-subscription resource discovery and resource group scoping.
Provides KQL patterns for orphaned resources, missing tags, and public access checks.
Formats read-only Azure CLI query results into user-friendly summaries.
Explains common Resource Graph errors and how to narrow query scope.
limitations: It does not create, update, deploy, or delete Azure resources.
It depends on the user having Azure CLI, MCP tools, and read permissions configured.
Azure Resource Graph data can lag behind real-time resource state.
It is not intended for non-Azure clouds or application code generation.

use_cases.title: Build A Resource Inventory
use_cases.description: List resources by type, subscription, region, or resource group for planning and operational reviews.

use_cases.title: Find Cleanup Candidates
use_cases.description: Identify unattached disks, unused public IP addresses, idle load balancers, and orphaned network interfaces.

use_cases.title: Check Governance Coverage
use_cases.description: Find resources missing required tags or resources with public network access enabled.

prompt_templates.title: List Basic Resources
prompt_templates.prompt: List my Azure resources by type and location. Limit the results so I can review the summary first.

prompt_templates.title: Scope To A Resource Group
prompt_templates.prompt: Show all resources in the resource group <resource-group-name>. Include name, type, location, and SKU when available.

prompt_templates.title: Find Orphaned Assets
prompt_templates.prompt: Find unattached managed disks and unused public IP addresses across my selected subscriptions. Explain why each result may be unused.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Tags And Exposure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Azure Resource Graph to find resources missing Environment or CostCenter tags and resources with public network access enabled. Group the findings by subscription and resource type.

output_examples.input: List all Azure resources by type.
output_examples.output: A grouped inventory summary showing each resource type, count, and the subscriptions where those resources appear.

output: Unattached network interfaces with resource group and location.
Public IP addresses with no IP configuration attached.
Load balancers that have no backend pools.

output_examples.input: Which resources are missing required tags?
output_examples.output: A governance report listing resource names, types, resource groups, and the missing tag categories.

best_practices: Review generated Azure CLI commands before running them in a tenant.
Scope broad searches with subscriptions, resource groups, or result limits.
Treat resource names, tags, and topology details as sensitive operational data.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to deploy, modify, or delete Azure resources.
Do not run broad tenant-wide queries when a narrow subscription scope is enough.
Do not paste query results that contain secrets or sensitive business identifiers.

faq.question: Does this skill change Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for read-only lookup and inventory workflows.

faq.question: What Azure service does it use for broad searches?
faq.answer: It uses Azure Resource Graph patterns when dedicated MCP tools do not cover the resource type.

faq.question: Can it search across subscriptions?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports cross-subscription discovery when the active account has read access.

faq.question: Does it require Azure permissions?
faq.answer: Yes. The user needs appropriate Azure read permissions, usually Reader access for the target scope.

faq.question: Can it help find unused resources?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for unattached disks, unused public IP addresses, and orphaned network interfaces.

faq.question: Is Azure Resource Graph real time?
faq.answer: No. Resource Graph is near real time, so very recent changes may not appear immediately.
